summary: Patrick McKenzie interviews salary negotiation expert Josh Doody about tactical approaches to negotiating compensation at every stage of employment. They cover how to deflect the 'what is your current salary' question without lying, why you should always negotiate upward from any offer, how to trade across multiple compensation axes (salary, vacation, equity), and how to build a formal written case for raises at your current company. The conversation emphasizes that negotiation behavior signals competence to employers — confident professionals who hold boundaries are perceived as stronger candidates. McKenzie estimates his original salary negotiation blog post has generated over $2.3 million in added annual salary for readers.
key_points: Never disclose your current salary — it caps your negotiating range and costs you compounding losses over your entire career, potentially $100,000+
Always counter-offer no matter how good the initial number sounds, because you don't know the employer's maximum willingness to pay
How you negotiate signals your professional competence — employers respect candidates who hold boundaries and distrust those who fold under pressure
When negotiating raises internally, put your case in writing with specific accomplishments and a target number so your manager can circulate it through the approval chain
If you can't get a raise, determine whether the firm can't afford it or you've topped out your pay grade — the solution may be pursuing a promotion instead
money_quotes: This question doesn't sound like, 'Hey, I'm trying to cheat you out of $100,000' but they're trying to cheat you out of $100,000 when they ask this, and they know this.
No matter what their offer is, I will negotiate upwards.
If you perform wishy-washy in the negotiation, they count it as a strike against you.
Don't work for people who don't appreciate what you bring to the table.

Your whole goal is to essentially make them a little bit uncomfortable, push them right up to the tippy-top of their comfort zone so that you know that they're giving you the best possible salary that they can give.
Professionals, when they want something done, they put it in writing. That's a core professional skill.
I think that people who roll over immediately are less trustworthy in their eyes than people who don't. Don't roll over immediately in the face of opposition.
If they don't believe you're an honest professional, then get out of this conversation, because you definitely don't want to be working there.
key_insight: Salary negotiation is not adversarial — it's a professional competence test where holding your boundaries and refusing to volunteer information signals exactly the kind of confident, trustworthy employee companies want to hire.
